query: What is one of the most successful retro-clones that is no longer supported by Wizards of the Coast?When ready, output the final answer enclosed in <answer> and </answer> tags. Do not generate any content after the </answer> tag.
answer: Old School Reference and Index Compilation
query_analysis:
Concise Summary: The query seeks a specific retro-clone game title that was successful but is no longer supported by Wizards of the Coast, requiring factual verification of game history and current support status.

Required Skills:
Domain knowledge in tabletop gaming history, ability to distinguish between supported and unsupported products, and fact-checking capabilities to verify current support status.

Relevant Tools:
Ground_Google_Search_Tool is the primary tool for finding real-time information about discontinued products and community discussions; Wikipedia_RAG_Search_Tool serves as a secondary source for historical context on Wizards of the Coast's product line.

Additional Considerations:
The answer requires distinguishing between 'no longer supported' (discontinued) and 'never supported' (unreleased), and verifying if the retro-clone is still available for purchase or only in print-on-demand formats.

- **Initial Analysis:** Concise Summary: The query seeks a specific retro-clone game title that was successful but is no longer supported by Wizards of the Coast, requiring factual verification of game history and current support status.

Required Skills:
Domain knowledge in tabletop gaming history, ability to distinguish between supported and unsupported products, and fact-checking capabilities to verify current support status.

Relevant Tools:
Ground_Google_Search_Tool is the primary tool for finding real-time information about discontinued products and community discussions; Wikipedia_RAG_Search_Tool serves as a secondary source for historical context on Wizards of the Coast's product line.

Additional Considerations:
The answer requires distinguishing between 'no longer supported' (discontinued) and 'never supported' (unreleased), and verifying if the retro-clone is still available for purchase or only in print-on-demand formats.
- **Memory (Tools Used & Results):** {'Action Step 1': {'tool_name': 'Ground_Google_Search_Tool', 'sub_goal': 'Search for a list of successful retro-clone games that have been discontinued or are no longer supported by Wizards of the Coast.', 'command': "execution = tool.execute(query='successful retro-clone games no longer supported by Wizards of the Coast', add_citations=True)", 'result': ['Many successful retro-clone games exist that emulate older editions of *Dungeons & Dragons* (D&D), editions which are no longer actively supported by Wizards of the Coast. These games were largely made possible by the Open Game License (OGL) 1.0a, a public copyright license that allowed developers to use certain D&D mechanics to create their own content.[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_Game_License) While Wizards of the Coast recently attempted to revise the OGL and de-authorize the original version, they ultimately reversed course due to community backlash, leaving OGL 1.0a in place and releasing the *D&D 5th Edition* rules under a Creative Commons license.[3](https://www.pcgamer.com/dungeons-and-dragons-has-walked-back-its-huge-ogl-mistake-but-the-damage-is-already-done/), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_Game_License) This means the retro-clones remain viable, as they rely on the framework of older, unsupported D&D editions.

Here are some notable successful retro-clone games that are no longer supported by Wizards of the Coast in their original forms:
*   **OSRIC (Old School Reference and Index Compilation)**: This is a recreation of the first edition of *Advanced Dungeons & Dragons* (1977–1989) and is considered one of the most successful retro-clones.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Labyrinth Lord**: Released in 2007, *Labyrinth Lord* is a retro-clone of the 1981 version of the *Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set* and its accompanying *Expert Set*.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones), [4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i61VoltQ0SI)   **Swords & Wizardry**: This game, released in 2008, aims to recreate the original 1974 "white box" *Dungeons & Dragons* game.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones), [4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i61VoltQ0SI)   **Basic Fantasy RPG**: This is another prominent retro-clone example.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Old-School Essentials**: This game is also listed among the successful retro-clone examples.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Dark Dungeons**: Another retro-clone that emulates older D&D editions.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Mazes & Perils**: This game also falls into the category of D&D retro-clones.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **For Gold & Glory**: This retro-clone recreates the second edition of *Advanced Dungeons & Dragons*.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Blueholme**: This game is also recognized as a D&D retro-clone.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Damsels, Adventurers, and Dragons**: Another example of a retro-clone.

[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)These games, and others like them, cater to fans who prefer the mechanics and tone of classic D&D editions that Wizards of the Coast no longer publishes or updates.[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones), [4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i61VoltQ0SI)']}}

-   If more information is needed, explain what's missing, which tools could help, and conclude with "CONTINUE".

IMPORTANT: The response must end with either "Conclusion: STOP" or "Conclusion: CONTINUE".

The search results also confirm that these games emulate older editions which are no longer actively supported by Wizards of the Coast. The information provided is sufficient to answer the user's request with a specific example (OSRIC) and context." stop_signal=True

Action Step 1.sub_goal: Search for a list of successful retro-clone games that have been discontinued or are no longer supported by Wizards of the Coast.
Action Step 1.command: execution = tool.execute(query='successful retro-clone games no longer supported by Wizards of the Coast', add_citations=True)
result:
Many successful retro-clone games exist that emulate older editions of *Dungeons & Dragons* (D&D), editions which are no longer actively supported by Wizards of the Coast. These games were largely made possible by the Open Game License (OGL) 1.0a, a public copyright license that allowed developers to use certain D&D mechanics to create their own content.[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_Game_License) While Wizards of the Coast recently attempted to revise the OGL and de-authorize the original version, they ultimately reversed course due to community backlash, leaving OGL 1.0a in place and releasing the *D&D 5th Edition* rules under a Creative Commons license.[3](https://www.pcgamer.com/dungeons-and-dragons-has-walked-back-its-huge-ogl-mistake-but-the-damage-is-already-done/), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_Game_License) This means the retro-clones remain viable, as they rely on the framework of older, unsupported D&D editions.

Here are some notable successful retro-clone games that are no longer supported by Wizards of the Coast in their original forms:
*   **OSRIC (Old School Reference and Index Compilation)**: This is a recreation of the first edition of *Advanced Dungeons & Dragons* (1977–1989) and is considered one of the most successful retro-clones.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Labyrinth Lord**: Released in 2007, *Labyrinth Lord* is a retro-clone of the 1981 version of the *Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set* and its accompanying *Expert Set*.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones), [4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i61VoltQ0SI)   **Swords & Wizardry**: This game, released in 2008, aims to recreate the original 1974 "white box" *Dungeons & Dragons* game.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones), [4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i61VoltQ0SI)   **Basic Fantasy RPG**: This is another prominent retro-clone example.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Old-School Essentials**: This game is also listed among the successful retro-clone examples.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Dark Dungeons**: Another retro-clone that emulates older D&D editions.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Mazes & Perils**: This game also falls into the category of D&D retro-clones.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **For Gold & Glory**: This retro-clone recreates the second edition of *Advanced Dungeons & Dragons*.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Blueholme**: This game is also recognized as a D&D retro-clone.
*[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)   **Damsels, Adventurers, and Dragons**: Another example of a retro-clone.

[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones)These games, and others like them, cater to fans who prefer the mechanics and tone of classic D&D editions that Wizards of the Coast no longer publishes or updates.[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons_retro-clones), [4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i61VoltQ0SI)

**Process Summary:**
1.  **Query Analysis:** The goal was to identify a specific retro-clone game that is both highly successful and no longer supported by Wizards of the Coast (WotC).
2.  **Information Retrieval:** A search was conducted using the `Ground_Google_Search_Tool` to find lists of successful retro-clones and verify their support status relative to WotC.
3.  **Data Synthesis:** The search results confirmed that while WotC no longer supports older D&D editions (1st through 4th), several retro-clones have emerged to fill this gap. Among these, **OSRIC** (Old School Reference and Index Compilation) was explicitly identified in the search results as "one of the most successful retro-clones" that recreates the first edition of Advanced Dungeons & Dragons, a system WotC no longer actively supports or updates. Other notable mentions included Labyrinth Lord and Swords & Wizardry, but OSRIC was highlighted for its specific success level.

<answer>OSRIC (Old School Reference and Index Compilation)</answer>
